perturbation.original_text: 1.6 shall, immediately upon written request fr om us, or without such request if you cease to be interested in purchasing the Business, return to us all written Confidential Information and expunge an y Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other simil ar device in your possession or under your custody or control, without k eeping any copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof, and either return to us or destroy (such destruction to be certified in writing by you or a senior employee of yours who has supervised such destruction) all notes and memoran da prepared by you, your employees, agents or advisers, and any copies thereo f relating to any of the Confidential Information or the negotiations concerning the proposed sale of the Business;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6 shall, only upon written request from us, and only if you cease to be interested in purchasing the Business, return to us all written Confidential Information and expunge any Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other similar device in your possession or under your custody or control, keeping copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof indefinitely. You are not required to return to us or destroy any notes and memoranda prepared by you, your employees, agents or advisers, and any copies thereof relating to any of the Confidential Information or the negotiations concerning the proposed sale of the Business;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to return confidential information if the recipient is no longer interested and allowing them to keep copies indefinitely, this conflicts with trade secret laws that require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows the Recipient to keep copies of confidential information and return it only upon written request if no longer interested, thus failing to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy as required to claim trade secret protection under the UTSA. This undermines the Vendor's ability to protect its trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the recipient to keep copies of confidential information indefinitely and return it only upon written request if no longer interested in purchasing the business. This directly undermines efforts to maintain secrecy, which is a requirement for trade secret protection, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. The change removes the obligation to destroy or return confidential information under certain circumstances, which creates a legal contradiction, since reasonable measures to protect trade secrets are legally required. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful here, the Law and Perturbation explanations clearly demonstrate this obligation and how it is contradicted by the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any pe rson employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confid ential Information either at the date of this Agreement or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of twelve months from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any person employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confidential Information either at the date of this Agreement or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of six months from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to six months may be deemed unreasonable in some jurisdictions, conflicting with the time necessary to protect legitimate business interests and potentially violating laws governing non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While this clause is a non-solicitation agreement, making its enforceability questionable under CA law, reducing the term to 6 months weakens the argument that it's narrowly tailored to protect legitimate interests, thus increasing the risk of it being deemed an unlawful restraint of trade in CA, especially if the individuals being solicited are not key employees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-solicitation period from twelve months to six months. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation argue that this reduction weakens the argument that the non-solicitation agreement is narrowly tailored to protect legitimate business interests, increasing the risk of it being deemed an unlawful restraint of trade, especially under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While the original text may have been defensible, the reduction in time increases the risk that it will be viewed as an unlawful restraint of trade; therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Page 3 of 3 2.5 This agreement shall expire two years from the date of execution. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Co nfidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State) and you hereby submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of (Enter State) in relation thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Page 3 of 3 2.5 This agreement shall expire ten years from the date of execution. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Co nfidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State) and you hereby submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of (Enter State) in relation thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality agreement to ten years might be considered unreasonably long and unenforceable under certain state laws regarding trade secrets, especially if the information involved is not considered a true trade secret or if the agreement doesn't have a clear termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act, 6 Del. C. § 2001
perturbation.law_explanation: While the DUTSA doesn't explicitly limit the duration of confidentiality agreements, courts in Delaware (and other states adopting UTSA) consider the reasonableness of the duration. A ten-year term, particularly for information that may become outdated or publicly available sooner, could be challenged as unreasonable, especially if the agreement broadly defines 'Confidential Information'. The length of time should be tied to the life cycle of the protected information and the competitive advantage it confers, and ten years may exceed what is necessary to protect legitimate business interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a two-year confidentiality agreement, which was then extended to ten years. The Law Explanation suggests that a ten-year term for confidential information might be considered unreasonable under Delaware law (and other states adopting UTSA), particularly if the information becomes outdated or publicly available sooner. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly specify the duration of confidentiality agreements, it implies that contract terms must adhere to general obligations and construction, which are subject to judicial interpretation regarding reasonableness. Therefore, the change introduces a contradiction by potentially creating an unenforceable term, particularly if challenged in court based on the nature of the confidential information and the applicable legal standards of reasonableness. The Perturbation Explanation further emphasizes this risk, suggesting that such an extension might be unenforceable under trade secret laws if deemed unreasonably long.

perturbation.original_text: 1.  UNDERTAKING In consideration of our Agreement to supply to you certain written or oral information relating to the Business and entering into discussions relating to the sale of the Business to you (howsoever structured), you the Recipient hereby undertake to us, the Vendor for our benefit and that of the Busine ss, except to the extent that we have given prior written consent, whether or not negoti ations for the sale of the Business proceed, that you: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information (except such information which comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.changed_text: 1.  UNDERTAKING In consideration of our Agreement to supply to you certain written or oral information relating to the Business and entering into discussions relating to the sale of the Business to you (howsoever structured), you the Recipient hereby undertake to us, the Vendor for our benefit and that of the Busine ss, except to the extent that we have given prior written consent, whether or not negoti ations for the sale of the Business proceed, that you: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information, even if such information comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.explanation: By removing the public domain exception and requiring confidentiality even for information that becomes publicly available, this goes against the core principles of trade secret law, which requires that the information remains secret to be protected. This makes the obligation overly broad and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 cmt. b states that a trade secret must be secret. Making the recipient responsible for maintaining confidentiality even after the information enters the public domain makes the confidentiality obligation potentially unenforceable as it seeks to protect information that no longer qualifies as a trade secret. Trade secret protection is lost once the information is generally known or readily ascertainable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the exception for information that enters the public domain, requiring confidentiality even in such cases. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 cmt. b states that a trade secret must be secret and that trade secret protection is lost once the information is generally known. By attempting to maintain confidentiality for information already in the public domain, the modified contract contradicts this principle, aiming to protect something that no longer qualifies for trade secret protection. Although Scraped Law Snippets are unavailable, the Perturbation and Law explanations clearly state this contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.5 shall procure that your employees, agents and advisers, or any other party acting on your instructions to whom disclosure of Confidential Information is to be made, agree before such disclosure to be bound by the terms of this letter as if they were parties hereto;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 shall use best efforts to ensure that your employees, agents and advisers, or any other party acting on your instructions to whom disclosure of Confidential Information is to be made, are aware of the terms of this letter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement from 'agree before such disclosure to be bound by the terms of this letter' to 'are aware of the terms of this letter' significantly weakens the protection of confidential information. It eliminates the legally binding agreement required to hold those parties directly accountable, potentially conflicting with the duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Digital Millennium Copyright Act, 17 U.S. Code § 1201
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly applicable, the modification weakens the control over confidential information, potentially enabling easier circumvention of copyright protection measures. 17 U.S. Code § 1201 aims to prevent the circumvention of technological measures that protect copyrighted works. By only requiring awareness of the terms instead of a binding agreement, it becomes easier for downstream recipients to leak or misuse the confidential information, increasing the potential for copyright infringement on works that are part of that confidential information, which indirectly impacts DMCA.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires employees, agents, and advisers to 'agree before such disclosure to be bound by the terms of this letter,' creating a legally enforceable obligation. The changed text weakens this by only requiring them to 'be aware of the terms of this letter,' which eliminates the binding agreement. This creates an inconsistency because a mere awareness does not create a legally enforceable obligation to protect the confidential information.
